ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Rempeyek; mapped to the nearest parent class 'cracker (food product)' as a defensible approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Rempeyek; A0EJX (Crackers and crispbreads) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Rempeyek or equivalent traditional Southeast Asian cracker category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Rempeyek is approximately 25 grams, equivalent to about 4–6 individual crackers, providing roughly 128 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo data for traditional deep-fried rice flour snacks. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for peanut-based crackers (FDC ID 172429) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for Southeast Asian snacks, as HPB does not publish full micronutrient panels for this item. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried rice flour products with coconut milk and peanuts.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Rice-based crackers and snack foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Rempeyek exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy to rice-based crackers and puffed rice snacks (GI range 55–72 in Atkinson et al. 2021). The presence of peanuts (protein and fat content) and deep-frying in coconut oil moderates the glycemic response relative to plain rice crackers. Glycemic load per 25 g serving calculated as (60 × 43.5 × 0.25) / 100 = 6.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg per 100 g) from dried anchovies and added salt, combined with high total fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit Rempeyek intake; a single 25 g serving contributes approximately 113 mg sodium (~5% of the 2,000 mg daily limit), and regular consumption of multiple servings can meaningfully elevate daily sodium load and blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 60) from rice flour base, with moderating effect from peanut protein and fat; high caloric density (510 kcal/100 g) poses portion-control risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Rempeyek to small portions (≤25 g per occasion) and avoid pairing with other high-GI foods, as the medium GI and high energy density can contribute to postprandial glucose excursions and excess caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (32.2 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g) from coconut milk and deep-frying oil, plus elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Rempeyek as an occasional food only; its high saturated fat and sodium content can adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure, and regular consumption is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation and refined carbohydrates may promote pro-inflammatory pathways; peanuts may trigger sensitivity in some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit deep-fried snacks like Rempeyek and monitor for peanut-related symptom flares, as both the frying process and refined starch content can contribute to systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rempeyek (also known as Peyek) is a traditional deep-fried savory cracker originating from Java, Indonesia, and widely popular across Malaysia and Singapore. It is made from a thin, seasoned batter of rice flour and coconut milk, flavored with ground coriander, cumin, turmeric, and garlic, then studded with whole peanuts and small dried anchovies (ikan bilis) before being deep-fried in hot oil until paper-thin, exceptionally crisp, and golden brown. The result is a light, shatteringly crunchy cracker with a savory, slightly nutty, and aromatic flavor profile. Variants include versions with dried shrimp or mung beans in place of or alongside peanuts and anchovies. Nutritionally, Rempeyek is energy-dense at approximately 510 kcal per 100 g, with a notable fat content of 32.2 g (including ~9.8 g saturated fat from coconut milk and frying oil), 12.5 g protein from peanuts and anchovies, and 43.5 g carbohydrate from rice flour. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg per 100 g due to the anchovies and seasoning. It has a medium glycemic index (~60) moderated by its fat and protein content. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat levels, and a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium GI and high caloric density. Rempeyek is a staple festive snack during Hari Raya Aidilfitri and is commonly served as a crunchy accompaniment to rice-based dishes such as Nasi Ambeng and Lontong. It is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most commercial preparations.
common_user_queries: What are the ingredients in Rempeyek?
How much sodium is in Rempeyek?
What is the glycemic index of Rempeyek?
meal_context: festive snack during Hari Raya Aidilfitri

confidence_reason: Core macros are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo data for comparable deep-fried rice flour snacks. Micronutrient values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as HPB does not publish a full micronutrient panel for Rempeyek specifically. GI is an analogous estimate from rice-based cracker data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from USDA data for comparable deep-fried coconut milk and peanut products. No single approved source provides an exact Rempeyek-specific full nutrient profile.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for deep-fried Rempeyek is not reported in HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables for this specific preparation as of April 2026. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace): rice flour, coconut milk, peanuts, and dried anchovies contribute no meaningful vitamin C; value confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. Saturated fat (9.8 g), monounsaturated fat (12.4 g), polyunsaturated fat (7.6 g), trans fat (0.1 g), and cholesterol (18.0 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for deep-fried peanut-based rice crackers with coconut milk, as HPB does not publish fatty acid fractions for this item. 4. The input source cited ('Journal of Food Composition and Analysis' via ScienceDirect) is not on the Project Veritas approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; all nutrient values are re-anchored to HPB, USDA FoodData Central, and FAO/INFOODS SEA. 5. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on rice-based crackers in Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL Values; no direct Rempeyek GI measurement is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (60 × 43.5 × 0.25) / 100 = 6.5 g for a 25 g serving. 7. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the input has been removed as Rempeyek contains animal-derived ingredients (dried anchovies, fish). 8. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches to the cracker/crisp category; no exact ontology code exists for Rempeyek in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of April 2026.
